original_abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) trained on massive code corpora have significantly advanced the field of code intelligence. However, current models often face two critical limitations: they are typically constrained to a single architecture (such as encoder-only or decoder-only) that may not be optimal for all applications, and they rely on a narrow set of pretraining objectives that may not generalize well across diverse downstream tasks. To overcome these hurdles, we introduce CodeT5+, a versatile family of encoder-decoder LLMs designed for code. CodeT5+ features a modular design that allows its components to be flexibly reconfigured to suit the specific requirements of various tasks.

This architectural flexibility is supported by a diverse mixture of pretraining objectives, including span denoising, contrastive learning, text-code matching, and causal language modeling, applied to both unimodal and bimodal multilingual datasets. To scale effectively without the prohibitive cost of training from scratch, we initialize CodeT5+ using frozen, off-the-shelf LLMs and further employ instruction-tuning to enhance alignment with human intent. We evaluated CodeT5+ across more than 20 benchmarks in zero-shot, fine-tuning, and instruction-tuning settings. Our results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across a wide spectrum of tasks. Notably, our instruction-tuned CodeT5+ 16B model achieves a new state-of-the-art on the HumanEval code generation benchmark with 35.0% pass@1 and 54.5% pass@10, outperforming other open-source code LLMs and even surpassing OpenAI's code-cushman-001.
humanized_abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) pre-trained on large codebases have recently shown impressive results on code intelligence tasks. While those models have a number of benefits, they suffer from two main drawbacks: (1) they are almost always limited to a fixed model architecture (such as encoder or decoder) which might not be the most efficient for each task, (2) they are only pre-trained on a fixed set of tasks (such as language modeling) which is not optimal since each task has its own specifics. To alleviate those issues, we introduce CodeT5+, a new family of encoder-decoder Large Language Models (LLMs) for code. CodeT5+ models are highly modular and their components can be easily swapped depending on the task at hand.

Through this work we show that, through carefully combining a wide range of pretraining objectives such as span denoising, contrastive learning, text-code matching and causal language modeling on unimodal and bimodal multilingual data, and leveraging the architectural flexibility provided by the T5 model, we can achieve strong performance on a large number of programming tasks. Training such models from scratch is computationally expensive and so to train CodeT5+ in a computationally efficient manner, we leverage pre-trained large language models (LLMs) in a freezing mode and perform instruction-tuning to match the intent of the human user. We conduct experiments across over 20 programming tasks in the zero-shot, few-shot, and instruction-tuning settings. Experimental results show that CodeT5+ achieves state-of-the-art results on almost all the benchmark tasks. Moreover, our instruction-tuned CodeT5+ 16B model reaches a new state-of-the-art on the HumanEval code generation benchmark with a pass@1 of 35.0% and pass@10 of 54.5%, surpassing all current open-source code LLMs, as well as the OpenAI’s code-cushman-001 model.

While those models have a number of benefits, they suffer from two main drawbacks: (1) they are almost always limited to a fixed model architecture (such as encoder or decoder) which might not be the most efficient for each task, (2) they are only pre-trained on a fixed set of tasks (such as language modeling) which is not optimal since each task has its own specifics. document.input:
Large Language Models (LLMs) trained on massive code corpora have significantly advanced the field of code intelligence.
